response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I believe that the best way to handle fake content is to be careful and not click on anything suspicious. I have lived through many changes, so my view of the world has taught me to question things. Looking at social media only occasionally helps because it gives you time to think clearly.

As a retired man with anxiety, I find scrolling too much can make me feel overwhelmed. So when I see something that seems off—like an ad promising quick money or messages from unknown people—I stop and think twice. My parents had lower and upper secondary education in Milan, so they taught me to read between the lines.

Religion has been a big part of my life since moving here as an immigrant. It helps remind me that not everything online is true; sometimes you have to rely on trusted sources. I also enjoy gardening and hiking, which means staying grounded keeps me focused on reality.

Using tools like reporting features or fact-checking websites can be useful, but they take effort. Since my anxiety makes me hesitant to dive too deep into the internet's rabbit hole, I stick to verified accounts and limit time spent online.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I am an elderly retired man with anxious tendencies living in a high-tech city like Milan. This requires cautious thinking about digital content.

My age brings perspective: fake information has been around throughout history but now spreads instantly through technology. My anxiety makes me wary of constant exposure, so managing it involves active caution and limiting engagement.

My background as an immigrant with Islamic beliefs emphasizes reliance on trusted sources rather than random online noise. Hobbies like gardening connect me to tangible reality versus virtual claims.",
  "tone": "Reflective",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I believe that the best way to handle fake content is to be careful and not click on anything suspicious. I have lived through many changes, so my view of the world has taught me to question things. Looking at social media only occasionally helps because it gives you time to think clearly.

As a retired man with anxiety, I find scrolling too much can make me feel overwhelmed. So when I see something that seems off—like an ad promising quick money or messages from unknown people—I stop and think twice. My parents had lower and upper secondary education in Milan, so they taught me to read between the lines.

Religion has been a big part of my life since moving here as an immigrant. It helps remind me that not everything online is true; sometimes you have to rely on trusted sources. I also enjoy gardening and hiking, which means staying grounded keeps me focused on reality.

Using tools like reporting features or fact-checking websites can be useful, but they take effort. Since my anxiety makes me hesitant to dive too deep into the internet's rabbit hole, I stick to verified accounts and limit time spent online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I am an elderly retired man with anxious tendencies living in a high-tech city like Milan. This requires cautious thinking about digital content.

My age brings perspective: fake information has been around throughout history but now spreads instantly through technology. My anxiety makes me wary of constant exposure, so managing it involves active caution and limiting engagement.

My background as an immigrant with Islamic beliefs emphasizes reliance on trusted sources rather than random online noise. Hobbies like gardening connect me to tangible reality versus virtual claims.
